active_question: What is the title of the photo collection produced by Minomura Akira and photographed by Tsukamoto Tetsuzō?
reason: A retained edited QA from this page already preserves Kawabata Tanako's more meaningful historical voice and significance.
support_evidence: By the end of 1952, Tanako had become the primary model for several sets of distributed bondage photographs sold through the magazine. Her work continued throughout 1953, including modeling for the photo collection Utsukushiki Shibari (“Beautiful Bondage”), produced by Minomura Akira and photographed by Tsukamoto Tetsuzō.

active_question: What is the Japanese term used to describe Ozuma's work as something rare, precious, and rarely shown?
edited_answer: something that is so rare, precious and valued that it is very rarely shown or shared with others
edited_question: What does mongai fushutsu mean in the description of Ozuma’s work?
reason: The edit makes the source's complete definition retrievable instead of testing only the Japanese label.
support_evidence: In 2009, Taiyotosho published a beautiful collection of Ozuma’s work which had been featured as an art exhibition at the Customs Museum (Fūzoku shiryōkan). The work exhibited by Ozuma is described as “mongai fushutsu” (門外不出) which is something that is so rare, precious and valued that it is very rarely shown or shared with others.

active_question: What is the alternative name for the Tuck Position, which is also known as the Racoon tie?
reason: The source directly pairs Tanuki shibari with the Tuck Position and Racoon-tie alias.
support_evidence: | Day 157: Tuck Position – Alt. Tanuki shibari (Racoon tie), Kakae Shibari Ryoukakae 抱縛り、両抱 (embrace tie with both arms) – Tie the wrists behind the knees to keep the leg up and the butt exposed. Try moving to different positions (on the back, facedown, side) to see how this influences the feeling of restriction and vulnerability. |

active_question: What specific tool is suggested for taking an average of 10 wraps of rope around a cylinder?
reason: A retained QA already teaches the ten-wrap averaging method; this tool-name lookup adds little distinct value.
support_evidence: Once that is complete, I plan to introduce better photos and a standardised measuring system so you can see exactly what you are getting. The best solution I have come across is to take an average of 10 wraps of rope around a cylinder with a vernier caliper. However, as people frequently misjudge sizes, I think it would be helpful to include a standard object like a pencil or major brand cigarette like Marlboro Red.

active_question: Which materials are described as very slippery, allowing for tying complex knots faster than high friction rope?
reason: The material identification is explicitly limited to Rope365's comparison of knot-tying friction and speed.
support_evidence: If you find you like the aesthetic of the damsel in distress, functional ties and/or techniques using a lot of knots, you might prefer to use cotton, nylon or MFP. Nylon/MFP are very slippery which allows tying complex knots faster than a high friction rope. Cotton has a medium grip which is a good compromise. They also come in a wide variety of colours and easy to dye.

active_question: What tool is suggested in Day 63 'Vibrations' as an alternative to using hands to wiggle the rope?
reason: The object lookup from one sensory exploration prompt is too narrow to improve general rope-bondage assistance.
support_evidence: Day 63: Vibrations – Try adding some vibration to your tie, either by using your hands to wiggle the rope or a vibrator. Try different location in the tie and notice how tension influence the propagation of vibration.

active_question: What Japanese term is often confused with rope anarchy and refers to a state of selflessness or self-effacement?
edited_answer: a state of selflessness or self-effacement
edited_question: What does muga (無我) mean in Kinbaku Today’s discussion of rope anarchy?
reason: The edit makes the source-defined meaning the answer and preserves the warning not to conflate it with rope anarchy.
support_evidence: This approach is the polar opposite and celebrates what is new, often uncritically. It is often used as a justification for people to do whatever they want with their rope, however they want to do it. It is often confused with things like “muga” (無我), which refers to a state of selflessness or self-effacement.

active_question: Which single column tie is described as having no bight and requiring two full pull-throughs of the rope?
edited_answer: we can’t untie from the bight or use it later on as an attachment point
edited_question: What tradeoff does Rope365 note when its lark’s-head single-column tie has no bight?
reason: The edit preserves the no-bight tradeoff instead of asking the model to identify a knot from incidental construction clues.
support_evidence: | Day 191: Lark’s Head – This single column tie is created from lark’s head followed by a reversed tension and a half hitch. It is very solid and will not collapse. Unlike most single column ties, it doesn’t have a bight, which is a good thing if we don’t want to have a piece of rope dangling for a cleaner look. This is also a trade, without the bight, we can’t untie from the bight or use it later on as an attachment point. This tie requires more time to tie as it requires two full pull-throughs of the rope. |

active_question: Which tie is described as a classic kidnapping pose where both wrists and both ankles are tied together?
reason: A four-limb restriction prompt supplies no circulation, nerve, consent, monitoring, or release guidance.
support_evidence: | Day 158: Beast Tie – Alt. kemono shibari 獣縛り (beast tie), forward-facing hogtie, all four together. Bring up both wrists and both ankles all tie together in this classic kidnapping pose. Try different movements on the ground to experiment how it influences the feeling of restriction and exposition. Inspirations and Resources

active_question: According to Day 62 'Dressing', what object should be passed between the skin and the rope to slide?
reason: The skin-and-rope sliding micro-step lacks friction-injury precautions and is grammatically incomplete as generated.
support_evidence: Day 62: Dressing – Explore with the dressing up the tension of the rope and the skin under it. Create a structure with wraps, pass a finger between the skin and the rope and slide. Compare different directions and note how the rope interacts with the skin.

active_question: What is the typical length range in meters for the second rope used in a two-ropes box tie?
reason: A body-dependent box-tie rope length is decontextualized from fit, anatomy, construction, and risk management.
support_evidence: This tie is often referred to as the two-ropes box tie, but the actual amount of rope used may vary depending on rope length and the shoulder width of the person being tied. Usually a second rope between 7-8 meters will be sufficient. Having a short rope to finish up may be handy.

active_question: What is described as the best way to discover rope because certain concepts require feeling and touching it?
reason: The recommendation is qualified by tactile learning needs, online access, critical thinking, and evolving safety knowledge.
support_evidence: Rope knowledge is infinite and there are many sources of information in various formats. In this section, you will find a variety of resources where you can learn more about rope bondage, shibari, kinbaku. Learning in person is the best way to discover rope as certain concepts requires to feel and touch the rope to understand the subtleties. That said, books, videos and other online content are great to explore the diversity of the rope world and can help get you started if you don’t have the privilege of a local community. Always look at the different resources with critical thinking. Rope is a rather recent practice in history and we are constantly learning to make it safer.

active_question: What is the title of the volume from 1954 that contains the two compelling images invited for reflection?
reason: The volume-title lookup comes from a personal reflection and adds no durable historical claim about the images.
support_evidence: I invite you to take a moment and reflect on two compelling images from 1954, Fuzoku Soshi, vol 1. Rope from a time when less was more.

active_question: Which book by Master K popularized the position that modern kinbaku inherits technical vocabulary from hojojutsu in English?
edited_answer: modern kinbaku inherits at least part of its technical vocabulary from hojojutsu
edited_question: What hojojutsu–kinbaku position did Master K’s The Beauty of Kinbaku popularize in English?
reason: The edit preserves the attributed historical position and avoids reducing a contested genealogy to a title quiz.
support_evidence: Different writers and practitioners have answered that question in very different ways. Some have emphasized continuity, arguing that Japanese rope bondage draws from a much older history of battlefield restraint, policing techniques, Edo-period punishment, and formalized capture or arresting rope. In this view, modern kinbaku inherits at least part of its technical vocabulary from hojojutsu, even if its purpose and context have changed dramatically. This general position was popularized in English by Master K’s The Beauty of Kinbaku, though the claim was considerably softened in the Japanese translation. A related version of the claim also appears in Midori’s The Seductive Art of Japanese Bondage, although Midori has substantially revised her own account since that book’s publication in 2002.

active_question: What specific school of thought did Yukimura consider the understanding of foundations and principles to be the essence of?
edited_answer: understanding the foundations and the principles
edited_question: What did Yukimura consider the essence of Yukimura Ryuu?
reason: The edit makes Yukimura's stated principle the answer instead of awkwardly asking for the school already implied by the question.
support_evidence: As Yukimura would say “Once you have learned the basics, please develop and enjoy your own style,” because for him, understanding the foundations and the principles were the essence of Yukimura Ryuu. The ties were a tool, a path, and means to find the heart of what he believed rope was about.

active_question: What is the name of the Japanese tie described as probably the most frequently taught and most frequently tied?
edited_answer: the attitude with which it is frequently taught
edited_question: Besides how often it is tied, what does Kinbaku Today say contributes to takate kote’s higher incident rate?
reason: The edit retains the source's safety-relevant teaching critique rather than another bare tie-name answer.
support_evidence: The takate kote is probably the most frequently taught and most frequently tied Japanese tie, both in the West and in Japan. That alone accounts for a higher rate of incidents. But so does the attitude with which it is frequently taught.

active_question: Which tie is also called Egyptian or sarcophagus tie because it reminds of the position of the famous mummies?
reason: The source directly pairs the descriptive aliases with the crossing-arms-on-chest position.
support_evidence: | Day 96: Crossing Arms on Chest – Aka zenpou takate shibari 前方高手縛り (elevated wrist in the front of the body). Also called Egyptian or sarcophagus tie because it reminds of the position of the famous mummies. Tie the wrists crossing each other over the chest. |

active_question: What pattern is created when inline cuffs are tied in a zig-zag pattern and then completed in the opposite direction?
reason: The named pattern is defined by its zig-zag cuff symmetry rather than presented as an unsupported load claim.
support_evidence: A mayfly pattern is created when we tie inline cuffs in a zig-zag pattern, then complete the symmetry in the opposite direction. Bonus: add crossing hitches when two ropes cross to add solidity to the structure.

active_question: What specific anatomical structure is mentioned as being moved away from to make the Open V Chest Harness more comfortable?
edited_answer: it moves the rope away from the clavicles and makes an angle that creates more natural pressure on the body
edited_question: Why does Rope365 say the Open V chest harness can be more comfortable?
reason: The edit preserves the complete source-attributed comfort rationale instead of testing one anatomical noun.
support_evidence: We can open the front stem to shape the chest in a different way. This variation can be more comfortable as it moves the rope away from the clavicles and makes an angle that creates more natural pressure on the body.

active_question: Which coiling method is described as easy, fast, and not adding unnecessary twists and stress on the rope for storage?
reason: The named coiling method is directly tied to a durable rope-storage benefit and complements the retained loose-knot precaution.
support_evidence: The overhand hank coiling method is easy, fast and doesn’t add unnecessary twists and stress on the rope for storage. If you want to be extra gentle with your ropes, you can keep the knot loose. This is a good idea for longer-term storage or more fragile natural rope than kinks easily when kept in a tight knot.

active_question: What is the alternative name for the tie where the forearms are tied horizontally in front in a box position?
reason: The colloquial, misspelled alias adds little beyond the position already described and omits practical safety context.
support_evidence: | Day 94: Reverse Box Tie – Aka straightjacket tie, front box tie, reversed takate kote shibari or reverse TK for short. Tie the forearms horizontally in front in a box position. This position is confortable, but challenging to tie in a restrictive way as the joints have a lot of mobility. Inspirations and Resources |

active_question: Who is the person whose work inspired the technique of pulling wraps up or down to change placement and tension?
reason: A bare inspiration attribution is less useful than the retained safety precaution from the same tie discussion.
support_evidence: Mixing wraps around the arms and around the chest allows to create different sensation and restriction. In particular, pulling the wraps up or down changes placement, tension, creates interesting new shapes and presses on the body in different ways. This is inspired by the work of Yukimura Haruki who was frequently using this kind of pattern.

active_question: Which style's techniques are described as being more minimalist in design and less focused on performance-based suspension and dynamic transitions?
edited_answer: the aesthetics of the stage show dominate the Akechi lineage, the Minomura approach focuses on the aesthetic of the woman
edited_question: How does Kinbaku Today contrast the aesthetics of the Akechi and Minomura lineages?
reason: The edit preserves the source's substantive lineage comparison rather than asking which style matches a single technical clue.
support_evidence: While the aesthetics of the stage show dominate the Akechi lineage, the Minomura approach focuses on the aesthetic of the woman. What is beautiful is not the rope itself, but what it reveals in the woman who is tied.
